trigger: a SORTED array where you need a pair or triple that sums to a target value
move: Put one pointer at each end and compare their sum to the target. If the sum is too big move the right pointer left; if too small move the left pointer right. Each step eliminates one candidate, giving O(n) total.
example: [2,7,11,15], target 9: l=2,r=15 sums 17>9 so r moves left; l=2,r=11 sums 13>9; l=2,r=7 sums 9 -> return [0,1]. For 3Sum fix one element then two-pointer the rest, skipping duplicates.
skeleton:
l,r=0,len(a)-1
while l<r:
  s=a[l]+a[r]
  if s==t: return [l,r]
  elif s<t: l+=1
  else: r-=1
gotcha: only works sorted; skip dupes for 3Sum or you emit repeated triplets
script: Sorted array, need a pair that hits a target? Don't brute force it. Put a finger on each end. Sum too big, pull the right finger in; too small, push the left one out. Every move kills a candidate, so it's one linear pass. Memory tag: sorted plus two fingers equals O of n.

trigger: find the longest or shortest contiguous subarray or substring satisfying a constraint
move: Expand the window by advancing the right pointer one element at a time, then shrink from the left while the window violates the constraint. Track the best valid window size seen so far. This avoids the O(n^2) brute force by reusing prior work.
example: Longest substring without repeating chars in 'abcabcbb': expand until duplicate 'a' found at index 3, then advance l past the first 'a' at index 0. Window 'bca' has length 3, which is the answer.
skeleton:
for r,x in enumerate(a):
  add x
  while bad: remove a[l]; l+=1
  best=max(best,r-l+1)
gotcha: defining 'bad' IS the problem — the constraint check is where all the thinking lives
script: Need the longest stretch meeting some rule? Expand right until you break it, then shrink from the left until you fix it. The window reuses prior work so you never restart from scratch. The trap: defining what makes a window bad is the real puzzle. Memory tag: grow right, shrink left, track best.

trigger: find two elements that sum to a target value and return their indices in one pass
move: Store each number's index in a hashmap as you iterate. For each new number, check if its complement (target - num) is already in the map. If so, you have your pair. This works in O(n) time because each lookup is O(1).
example: nums=[2,7,11,15], target=9: at index 0 store seen[2]=0; at index 1 check 9-7=2, found in seen -> return [0,1]. The complement is always checked before inserting so a number can't pair with itself.
gotcha: return indices not values; complement check must come BEFORE inserting the current number
script: Need two numbers that add to a target, in one pass? Store each number's index as you go. For every new number, ask if the thing it needs is already in the map. If yes, done. The trap: check the map before you insert or a number might pair with itself. Memory tag: seen the complement, return the pair.

trigger: detect a cycle in a linked list or find the middle node without knowing the length
move: Move fast two steps and slow one step per iteration. If there is a cycle, fast will lap slow and they will meet. If there is no cycle, fast reaches None first. When fast hits the end, slow is at the middle — useful for reversing the second half.
example: List 1->2->3->4->2 (cycle at node 2): after 3 iterations slow=3, fast=3, they meet — cycle detected. For middle of 1->2->3->4->5: slow stops at 3 when fast reaches 5.
skeleton:
slow=fast=head
while fast and fast.next:
  slow=slow.next; fast=fast.next.next
  if slow==fast: return True  # cycle
gotcha: middle of even-length list: slow lands at the second of the two middle nodes
script: Linked list with a possible cycle, or need the middle without knowing the length? Move one pointer one step and another two steps. If there is a cycle they will eventually meet. When fast hits the end, slow is at the middle. The trap: the middle of an even list lands on the second of the two center nodes. Memory tag: tortoise and hare.

trigger: reverse a singly linked list in-place with O(1) extra space
move: Walk the list with three pointers: prev starts at None, cur at head. Each iteration save cur.next before overwriting it, point cur.next to prev, then advance both. When cur is None, prev is the new head.
example: 1->2->3->None: iteration 1 rewires 1->None, iteration 2 rewires 2->1, iteration 3 rewires 3->2. Return prev which points to 3->2->1->None.
skeleton:
prev=None
while head:
  nxt=head.next; head.next=prev
  prev=head; head=nxt
return prev
gotcha: save nxt BEFORE rewiring or you lose the rest of the chain — the order is critical
script: Reverse a linked list in place, no extra space. Walk with three pointers: save next, flip the current arrow to point backwards, then advance. Do that until you run out of nodes and prev is your new head. The trap: save the next pointer before you rewire or you lose the rest of the chain. Memory tag: save, flip, advance.

trigger: find the next greater element or first warmer day for each element in an array
move: Push indices onto a stack in decreasing-value order. When you encounter a value larger than the stack top, the current index is the 'next greater' answer for every index you pop. Store indices (not values) so you can compute the distance between positions.
example: Daily temps [73,74,75,71,69,72]: push 0(73), then 74 beats 73 so pop 0: answer[0]=1-0=1. Push 1(74), then 75 beats 74: answer[1]=2-1=1. 71 and 69 push on. 72 beats 69: answer[4]=5-4=1, then beats 71: answer[3]=5-3=2.
skeleton:
stack=[]
res=[0]*len(t)
for i,v in enumerate(t):
  while stack and t[stack[-1]]<v:
    j=stack.pop()
    res[j]=i-j
  stack.append(i)
gotcha: store indices, not values, so you can compute the distance; residual stack entries get 0
script: Need the next bigger element for each position? Keep a stack of indices in decreasing order of their values. When a bigger value arrives, pop everything it beats and record the distance. Store indices not values so you can compute gaps. The trap: leftover items in the stack get zero. Memory tag: stack of waiting losers.

trigger: check whether a string of brackets and parentheses is properly balanced and nested
move: Push every opening bracket onto a stack. On a closing bracket, pop the top and verify it matches the expected opener via a lookup map. If the stack is empty on a close or non-empty at the end, the string is invalid.
example: s='([])': push '(', push '[', see ']' -> pop '[' matches, see ')' -> pop '(' matches, stack empty -> True. s='([)]': push '(', push '[', see ')' -> pop '[' != '(' -> False.
gotcha: empty stack on a close bracket is False; non-empty stack at the end is also False
script: Checking if brackets are balanced? Push every opener onto a stack. When you see a closer, pop and verify it matches using a lookup map. Empty stack on a close or non-empty at the end both mean invalid. The trap: both failure modes matter equally. Memory tag: push opens, pop and match closes.

trigger: a sorted array where you need to find a target or its insertion point in O(log n)
move: Maintain left and right boundaries; compute mid as l+(r-l)//2 to avoid integer overflow. If a[mid] equals the target return mid; if too small set l=mid+1; if too large set r=mid-1. The search space halves every step.
example: a=[1,3,5,7,9], t=7: l=0,r=4, mid=2 a[2]=5<7 so l=3; mid=3 a[3]=7==7 -> return 3. For t=6: eventually l>r, return -1 (not found).
skeleton:
l,r=0,len(a)-1
while l<=r:
  m=l+(r-l)//2
  if a[m]==t: return m
  elif a[m]<t: l=m+1
  else: r=m-1
return -1
gotcha: use l<=r not l<r; update to m+1/m-1 not m or you get infinite loops
script: Sorted array, find a target fast. Keep left and right boundaries, compute mid as left plus half the gap. If mid is too small move left up; too big move right down. Search space halves every step. The trap: use left less than or equal to right and always update to mid plus or minus one or you get an infinite loop. Memory tag: halve the space every round.

trigger: find the shortest path in a graph or traverse a tree level by level
move: Initialize a deque with the starting node. Pop from the left, process the node, and enqueue its unvisited neighbors. Because each node is processed in the order it was discovered, BFS guarantees the shortest hop count to each node.
example: Tree [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]: level 0 queue=[3]; pop 3, enqueue 9 and 20; level 1 queue=[9,20]; pop 9 (no children), pop 20 enqueue 15 and 7; level 2 queue=[15,7]. Result: [[3],[9,20],[15,7]].
gotcha: use deque not list — popleft() is O(1); list.pop(0) is O(n) and will TLE on large inputs
script: Find shortest path or traverse by level? Use a double-ended queue and pop from the left. Push all unvisited neighbors to the right. Because nodes come out in discovery order, the first time you reach a node is the shortest route. The trap: use a deque, not a plain list — popping from the front of a list is slow. Memory tag: queue gives shortest hop count.

trigger: compute tree depth, check path sums, or verify structural symmetry by visiting every node
move: Define a recursive function with a base case returning a neutral value (0, True, or []) when node is None. Recurse left and right, then combine the two results. The call stack handles backtracking automatically.
example: Max depth of [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]: dfs(3)=1+max(dfs(9),dfs(20))=1+max(1,2)=3. dfs(20)=1+max(dfs(15),dfs(7))=1+max(1,1)=2. Bottoms out when node is None returning 0.
gotcha: always check node is not None before accessing .left or .right — AttributeError on None is the classic mistake
script: Need to compute something over a whole tree? Write a recursive function that returns a neutral value when it hits a null node, then combines the left and right child results. The call stack handles backtracking automatically. The trap: always check for null before accessing left or right or you get an attribute error. Memory tag: base case null, recurse and combine.

trigger: generate all subsets, permutations, combinations, or valid bracket strings exhaustively
move: Choose an element, append it to the current path, recurse deeper, then remove it (unchoose). This explore-and-undo pattern systematically visits every valid candidate without keeping all of them in memory at once.
example: Subsets of [1,2,3]: bt(0,[]) records []; bt adds 1 -> bt(1,[1]) records [1]; adds 2 -> records [1,2]; adds 3 -> records [1,2,3]; backtracks to [1,3], then [2], [2,3], [3]. All 8 subsets captured.
gotcha: append path[:] (a copy), never the live list — appending path appends a reference that changes on the next pop
script: Generating all subsets or permutations? Choose an element, recurse deeper, then undo the choice. This explore-and-backtrack loop visits every valid candidate without holding them all in memory. The trap: append a copy of the current path, not the live list, or every recorded result changes when you backtrack. Memory tag: choose, recurse, unchoose.

trigger: find the maximum sum of any contiguous subarray in an array that may contain negatives
move: Track a running current sum. At each element decide whether to extend the current subarray or start fresh: cur=max(num, cur+num). Keep a global best. Starting fresh when cur would go negative is the key insight.
example: [-2,1,-3,4,-1,2,1,-5,4]: at 4 cur resets from -4 to 4; then 4,-1,2,1 accumulates to 6; at -5 cur drops to 1; best stays 6. Answer is 6 from subarray [4,-1,2,1].
skeleton:
cur=best=nums[0]
for n in nums[1:]:
  cur=max(n,cur+n)
  best=max(best,cur)
return best
gotcha: initialize cur and best to nums[0] not 0 — all-negative input like [-3,-1,-2] should return -1, not 0
script: Max sum of any contiguous slice? At each element decide whether to extend the running sum or start fresh — whichever is bigger. Keep a global best. Starting fresh when the running total goes negative is the key insight. The trap: seed current and best with the first element, not zero, so all-negative inputs return the least-bad element. Memory tag: extend or restart, track best.

trigger: answer multiple range sum queries or count subarrays meeting an exact sum target efficiently
move: Build a prefix array where pre[i] is the sum of the first i elements (0-indexed with a leading 0). The sum of elements from index l to r is pre[r+1]-pre[l]. This converts O(n) per query to O(1) after O(n) preprocessing.
example: a=[3,0,1,4,2], pre=[0,3,3,4,8,10]. Sum of indices 1..3 = pre[4]-pre[1] = 8-3 = 5. Check: 0+1+4=5. For subarray-sum-equals-K, track running sums and look up pre[i]-K in a hashmap.
skeleton:
pre=[0]*(len(a)+1)
for i,x in enumerate(a): pre[i+1]=pre[i]+x
# sum l..r: pre[r+1]-pre[l]
gotcha: the leading 0 in the prefix array is essential — without it index l=0 has no valid prior entry
script: Answering many range sum queries? Build a prefix array where each position holds the total so far. Sum from index left to right is just the prefix at right plus one minus prefix at left. The trap: include a leading zero in the prefix array or you have no valid entry for ranges starting at index zero. Memory tag: prefix zero, subtract to get range.

trigger: count the number of contiguous subarrays whose elements sum to exactly K
move: Use a running prefix sum and a hashmap of how many times each prefix sum has occurred. For each element, the number of valid subarrays ending here equals cnt[current_sum - k], because any prior prefix at that value produces a subarray summing to K.
example: nums=[1,1,1], k=2: after index 0 s=1, cnt={0:1,1:1}; after index 1 s=2, cnt[2-2]=cnt[0]=1 so res=1, cnt={0:1,1:1,2:1}; after index 2 s=3, cnt[3-2]=cnt[1]=1 so res=2. Answer: 2.
skeleton:
cnt=defaultdict(int); cnt[0]=1; s=0; res=0
for x in nums:
  s+=x
  res+=cnt[s-k]
  cnt[s]+=1
gotcha: initialize cnt[0]=1 to count subarrays that start at index 0 and hit K exactly
script: Count subarrays that sum to exactly K? Track a running prefix sum and a map of how many times each sum has appeared. For each step, the number of valid subarrays ending here equals how often you have seen running sum minus K before. The trap: seed the map with zero appearing once to count subarrays starting from the beginning. Memory tag: seen prefix minus K, count it.

trigger: find the element that appears more than n/2 times in a list using O(1) space
move: Maintain a candidate and a count. When you see the candidate increment the count; when you see anything else decrement it. When count hits 0 replace the candidate. The majority element always survives this elimination because it has more votes than all others combined.
example: nums=[2,2,1,1,1,2,2]: candidate=2 count=1; see 2: count=2; see 1: count=1; see 1: count=0; see 1: candidate=1 count=1; see 2: count=0; see 2: candidate=2 count=1. Return 2.
skeleton:
cand=count=0
for n in nums:
  if count==0: cand=n
  count+=1 if n==cand else -1
return cand
gotcha: only guaranteed correct if a majority element is certain to exist — add a verification pass if unsure
script: Find the majority element with no extra space? Keep a candidate and a vote count. Matching the candidate adds a vote; anything else cancels one. When count hits zero switch candidates. The majority element has more votes than all others combined so it always survives. The trap: only guaranteed correct if a majority element actually exists — add a verification pass if unsure. Memory tag: votes cancel, majority survives.

trigger: every element in an array appears exactly twice except one; find the singleton in O(n) time O(1) space
move: XOR every element together. Pairs cancel each other out because a^a=0, and XOR with 0 is identity (a^0=a). The lone unpaired element is all that remains.
example: [4,1,2,1,2]: 4^1=5, 5^2=7, 7^1=6, 6^2=4. Only 4 has no pair so it survives. Order doesn't matter because XOR is commutative and associative.
skeleton:
res=0
for n in nums: res^=n
return res
gotcha: order doesn't matter due to commutativity; this only works when exactly one element appears an odd number of times
script: Every element appears twice except one — find the singleton with no extra space? XOR everything together. Paired elements cancel out because anything XOR itself is zero. Only the unpaired element remains. The trap: this only works when exactly one element appears an odd number of times. Memory tag: pairs cancel, singleton survives.

trigger: check if two strings are anagrams or count character frequency for any comparison problem
move: Counter(s) builds a frequency map in O(n). Two strings are anagrams if and only if their Counter maps are equal. For problems that require ordering, sorted(s)==sorted(t) also works but is slower at O(n log n).
example: 'anagram' vs 'nagaram': Counter gives {'a':3,'n':1,'g':1,'r':1,'m':1} for both -> True. 'rat' vs 'car': Counter('rat')={'r':1,'a':1,'t':1}, Counter('car')={'c':1,'a':1,'r':1} -> not equal -> False.
gotcha: Counter is O(n); sorted is O(n log n) — use Counter unless the interviewer asks for a sort-based approach
script: Need to know if two strings are anagrams? Build a frequency map with Counter and compare them directly. Two strings match if and only if their character counts are identical. The trap: Counter is linear time; sorting both strings also works but is slower. Memory tag: same letter counts means anagram.

trigger: find the K most or least frequent elements, or the K largest/smallest values from a collection
move: Count frequencies with Counter, then use heapq.nlargest(k, cnt, key=cnt.get) to extract the top K keys. This runs in O(n log k) which beats a full sort at O(n log n) when k is small. For O(n), use bucket sort keyed by frequency.
example: nums=[1,1,1,2,2,3], k=2: Counter gives {1:3,2:2,3:1}; nlargest(2, cnt, key=cnt.get) returns [1,2]. Bucket sort: bucket[3]=[1], bucket[2]=[2], bucket[1]=[3], read top k from the right.
skeleton:
from collections import Counter
import heapq
cnt=Counter(nums)
return heapq.nlargest(k,cnt,key=cnt.get)
gotcha: nlargest/nsmallest are O(n log k) which is faster than sorting the full list when k << n
script: Find the K most frequent elements? Count everything with Counter, then ask for the top K keys ranked by their count. This is faster than sorting everything when K is much smaller than the total. The trap: if you need exactly O of n, use bucket sort keyed by frequency instead. Memory tag: count then grab top K.

trigger: given a list of intervals, merge all overlapping ones into a minimal non-overlapping set
move: Sort intervals by start time. Walk the sorted list and compare each interval's start to the current merged interval's end. If they overlap (start <= end), extend the end to the max of both ends. Otherwise push the new interval and start a fresh merge.
gotcha: use max() when merging ends because a smaller interval can be fully contained within the current one
script: Merge overlapping intervals from a list? Sort by start time. Walk through and compare each interval's start to the current merged end. Overlap means extend the end to the larger of the two; no overlap means push the current and start fresh. The trap: use max when merging ends because a short interval can be fully swallowed by a longer one. Memory tag: sort by start, extend or push.

trigger: count ways to reach step n taking 1 or 2 steps at a time — classic Fibonacci recurrence
move: The number of ways to reach step i equals ways(i-1) + ways(i-2), because you arrive from one step back or two steps back. You only need the last two values so you can use O(1) space instead of a full array.
example: n=4: ways=[1,1,2,3,5]; only keep rolling two vars: (a,b)=(1,1)->(1,2)->(2,3)->(3,5). Return 5. Matches Fibonacci: f(1)=1, f(2)=2, f(3)=3, f(4)=5.
skeleton:
a,b=1,1
for _ in range(n-1): a,b=b,a+b
return b
gotcha: base case: n=1 should return 1; seed a=b=1 before the loop starts
script: Count ways to reach step N taking one or two steps at a time? The count at each step equals the sum of the two steps before it — it is Fibonacci. You only ever need the last two values so track two variables and roll them forward. The trap: seed both variables at one before the loop. Memory tag: Fibonacci in disguise.

trigger: maximize the sum of non-adjacent elements from an array (no two selected elements can be neighbors)
move: At each house you choose: skip it (keep the previous best) or rob it (add its value to the best two houses ago). dp[i]=max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]+nums[i]). Track only two variables to achieve O(1) space.
skeleton:
a=b=0
for n in nums:
  a,b=b,max(b,a+n)
return b
gotcha: the tuple assignment a,b=b,max(b,a+n) updates both atomically — using separate lines breaks the logic
script: Max loot from houses where you cannot rob two in a row? At each house either skip it and keep the previous best, or rob it and add what you had two houses ago. Track just two variables and roll them forward. The trap: the swap must be atomic — updating one variable before using it in the same expression breaks the logic. Memory tag: skip or rob, never neighbors.

trigger: find the minimum number of coins to make an exact amount, with unlimited use of each coin denomination
move: Build a dp table where dp[i] is the fewest coins to make amount i. For each amount from 1 to target, try every coin and take the minimum. This works because each sub-problem only depends on smaller sub-problems already solved.
gotcha: initialize dp[0]=0 (zero coins for amount 0); return -1 if dp[amount] is still inf at the end
script: Fewest coins to reach an exact amount? Build a table where each position holds the best answer so far. For every amount from one up, try every coin and take whichever gives the smallest total. Depends only on smaller amounts already solved. The trap: seed amount zero as zero and return negative one if the final cell is still infinity. Memory tag: fill bottom up, each coin is an option.

trigger: return an array where each element is the product of all other elements, without using division
move: Two passes: left-to-right builds prefix products into the result array; right-to-left multiplies in suffix products with a running accumulator. Each position ends up holding the product of everything to its left times everything to its right.
skeleton:
res=[1]*len(nums)
for i in range(1,len(nums)): res[i]=res[i-1]*nums[i-1]
r=1
for i in range(len(nums)-1,-1,-1):
  res[i]*=r; r*=nums[i]
gotcha: the no-division constraint rules out the naive approach; two-pass is required
script: Product of everything except the current element, no division allowed? First pass left to right fills in everything to the left of each index. Second pass right to left multiplies in everything to the right. Two passes, constant extra space. The trap: the no-division constraint rules out the shortcut — two passes are required. Memory tag: left products then right products.

trigger: rotate an array right by k steps in-place without extra space
move: k%=n to handle over-rotation. Then reverse the whole array, reverse the first k elements, and reverse the remaining n-k elements. Three reversals achieve the rotation in O(n) time with O(1) space.
example: a=[1,2,3,4,5,6,7], k=3: k%=7=3; reverse all -> [7,6,5,4,3,2,1]; reverse first 3 -> [5,6,7,4,3,2,1]; reverse last 4 -> [5,6,7,1,2,3,4]. Correct: each element moved 3 places right.
skeleton:
def rev(l,r):
  while l<r: a[l],a[r]=a[r],a[l]; l+=1; r-=1
k%=len(a)
rev(0,len(a)-1); rev(0,k-1); rev(k,len(a)-1)
gotcha: always do k%=n first; if k equals n the first reverse call gets rev(0,-1) which is a no-op and still correct
script: Rotate an array right by K steps in place? First reduce K modulo the length. Then reverse the whole array, reverse the first K elements, then reverse the rest. Three reversals achieve the rotation with no extra space. The trap: always take K mod length first or the reversal indices go out of bounds. Memory tag: mod, then three reverses.

trigger: remove duplicate values from a sorted array in-place and return the count of unique elements
move: Use a write pointer w starting at 1. Walk a read pointer r through the array; whenever a[r] differs from a[w-1] write a[r] into position w and advance w. The judge only inspects the first w positions so elements after w are irrelevant.
skeleton:
if not a: return 0
w=1
for r in range(1,len(a)):
  if a[r]!=a[w-1]: a[w]=a[r]; w+=1
return w
gotcha: return w (the count), not the modified array; elements beyond index w-1 are ignored
script: Remove duplicates from a sorted array in place? Use a write pointer starting at one. Advance a read pointer and whenever it sees a new value, write it into the write position and advance the write pointer. The trap: return the write pointer count, not the full array — the judge ignores everything beyond that index. Memory tag: write on change, return count.

trigger: aggregate rows into groups and then filter which groups pass based on the aggregate result
move: WHERE filters individual rows before any grouping happens. GROUP BY collapses matching rows into one group per distinct value. HAVING filters those groups using aggregate expressions like COUNT or SUM. You cannot use aggregate functions in WHERE.
example: Find emails that appear more than once: SELECT email, COUNT(*) cnt FROM Person GROUP BY email HAVING COUNT(*) > 1. WHERE COUNT(*) > 1 would be a syntax error because aggregation hasn't happened yet.
skeleton:
SELECT col, COUNT(*) cnt
FROM t
GROUP BY col
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
gotcha: put aggregate conditions in HAVING not WHERE — WHERE runs before groups form and will throw an error
script: Counting something per group, then filtering the groups? WHERE filters rows before you aggregate; HAVING filters the groups after. That is the whole trick. The trap: you cannot put a COUNT or SUM in a WHERE clause. Memory tag: WHERE is before, HAVING is after.

trigger: compare a row to another row within the same table using a relationship like managerId
move: Alias the same table twice and join on the relationship column. The first alias represents the employee, the second represents the referenced entity (the manager). Then filter in WHERE as if they were two separate tables.
example: SELECT e.name FROM Employee e JOIN Employee m ON e.managerId=m.id WHERE e.salary > m.salary. This finds employees who out-earn their manager. Bob with salary 4000 whose manager has salary 3000 appears in the result.
skeleton:
SELECT a.name
FROM Employee a
JOIN Employee b ON a.managerId=b.id
WHERE a.salary > b.salary
gotcha: INNER JOIN silently drops employees with no manager (managerId IS NULL) — intended here but verify for other problems
script: Comparing a row to another row in the same table? Alias the table twice and join on the relationship column — one alias as the employee, one as the manager. Filter as if they were two separate tables. The trap: an inner join silently drops rows where the manager ID is null, which is often intentional but worth verifying. Memory tag: same table, two aliases, join on the relationship.

trigger: find rows in table A that have no corresponding row in table B — the 'never did X' pattern
move: Three approaches: NOT IN (simple but NULL-unsafe), NOT EXISTS (correlated, NULL-safe), or LEFT JOIN where the right side IS NULL (readable, NULL-safe). All produce the same result when the subquery has no NULLs, but NOT EXISTS is the safest default.
example: Customers who never placed an order: SELECT name FROM Customers c WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM Orders o WHERE o.customerId=c.id). If Orders.customerId has a NULL row, NOT IN would return zero results; NOT EXISTS is unaffected.
skeleton:
-- NOT EXISTS (safest):
SELECT name FROM Customers c
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM Orders WHERE customerId=c.id)
-- LEFT JOIN form:
SELECT c.name FROM Customers c
LEFT JOIN Orders o ON c.id=o.customerId
WHERE o.customerId IS NULL
gotcha: NOT IN returns zero rows if any value in the subquery is NULL — use NOT EXISTS to be safe
script: Find rows in one table that have no match in another? Use NOT EXISTS with a correlated subquery — it checks row existence and handles nulls safely. NOT IN breaks silently when the subquery contains any null value. Left join where the right side is null also works and is readable. Memory tag: NOT EXISTS is null-safe, NOT IN is not.

trigger: select the top N salaries or scores per department with ties included and no rank gaps
move: Wrap the table in a CTE using DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC) to assign ranks within each group. Then filter the outer query WHERE rk <= N. DENSE_RANK ensures ties share the same rank and the next distinct value gets the next sequential rank.
example: Top 3 salaries per department: if dept IT has salaries [90000,90000,80000,70000], DENSE_RANK gives [1,1,2,3]. WHERE rk<=3 returns all four rows (both rank-1 employees, the rank-2, and rank-3). RANK would give ranks [1,1,3,4] and miss the 80000.
skeleton:
WITH r AS (
  SELECT *, DENSE_RANK() OVER
    (PARTITION BY deptId ORDER BY salary DESC) rk
  FROM Employee)
SELECT * FROM r WHERE rk<=3
gotcha: DENSE_RANK has no gaps; RANK skips rank numbers after a tie, so the Nth distinct value might not have rank N
script: Top N salaries per department with ties included? Window the table with DENSE_RANK partitioned by department and ordered by salary descending. Then filter the outer query where rank is at most N. DENSE_RANK gives ties the same rank and never skips a number after a tie. Memory tag: DENSE_RANK plus partition plus filter by rank.

trigger: rank rows within a partition and need to choose which ranking function to use for ties
move: ROW_NUMBER gives every row a unique sequential integer regardless of ties. RANK gives tied rows the same number but then skips the next number (1,1,3). DENSE_RANK gives tied rows the same number and does not skip (1,1,2). Choose based on whether gaps and uniqueness matter.
example: Scores [100,100,90,80]: ROW_NUMBER gives [1,2,3,4]; RANK gives [1,1,3,4]; DENSE_RANK gives [1,1,2,3]. For 'second highest salary' use DENSE_RANK WHERE rk=2 to capture 90, not RANK WHERE rk=2 (which returns no rows).
skeleton:
SELECT name,
  ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY score DESC) rn,
  RANK()       OVER (ORDER BY score DESC) rk,
  DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY score DESC) dr
FROM scores
gotcha: for Nth distinct value always use DENSE_RANK; RANK can skip to N+1 or higher after ties
script: Need to rank rows but you are not sure which ranking function handles ties correctly? ROW_NUMBER gives every row a unique integer no matter what. RANK gives ties the same number then skips. DENSE_RANK gives ties the same number and never skips. The trap: for the Nth distinct value always use DENSE_RANK or RANK might jump past N. Memory tag: DENSE_RANK never skips, RANK does.

trigger: compute a cumulative sum partitioned by a category and ordered chronologically within each group
move: SUM(col) OVER (PARTITION BY grp ORDER BY date) accumulates values within each partition in date order. The implicit frame is ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING TO CURRENT ROW, so each row's value includes all prior rows in the partition.
example: Spending by gender ordered by date: male rows [100,200,50] on 2021-01-01,2021-01-02,2021-01-03 produce running totals [100,300,350]. The female partition accumulates independently. Without ORDER BY, every row would show the partition total instead.
skeleton:
SELECT id, date, amount,
  SUM(amount) OVER
    (PARTITION BY gender ORDER BY date) running_total
FROM spending
gotcha: omitting ORDER BY makes the window the entire partition — every row shows the grand total, not a running total
script: Need a running total that resets per group? Use SUM with an OVER clause, partition by your group column, and order by date. Each row then holds the cumulative sum for its group up to that point. The trap: if you omit the ORDER BY, every row shows the whole group total instead of a running sum. Memory tag: OVER with ORDER BY gives running, without gives total.

trigger: compare each row's value to the previous or next row's value without a self-join
move: LAG(col, 1) OVER (ORDER BY ...) returns the prior row's value in a new column. LEAD does the same for the next row. Filter in an outer query or WHERE clause to find rows where the comparison holds. No join needed, which makes it cleaner than a self-join on date arithmetic.
example: Rising temperature: LAG(temperature) OVER (ORDER BY recordDate) prev gives yesterday's temp inline. Then WHERE temperature > prev_temp in an outer query returns the ids of warmer days. Self-join on DATEDIFF=1 also works but fails if dates have gaps (two rows with same diff).
skeleton:
SELECT id, recordDate, temperature,
  LAG(temperature) OVER (ORDER BY recordDate) prev_temp
FROM Weather
gotcha: a self-join on DATEDIFF=1 can match the wrong row if dates have gaps; LAG on ORDER BY recordDate is safer
script: Compare each row to the row before it without a self join? LAG gives you the prior row's value as a new column in the current row. Wrap it in a subquery or CTE and filter on the comparison. The trap: a self join on date difference of one fails when dates have gaps because a different row might match instead. Memory tag: LAG is cleaner than self joining on date math.

trigger: count or sum rows by category in a single GROUP BY pass instead of multiple subqueries
move: Wrap a condition inside SUM(CASE WHEN ... THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) to count only rows matching that condition per group. This is effectively a pivot on a category column and avoids multiple scans of the same table.
example: Confirmation rate per user: SUM(CASE WHEN action='confirmed' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/COUNT(*) gives the fraction. If user 7 has 3 rows total and 2 confirmed, rate = 2/3 = 0.67. Wrap with ROUND(COALESCE(...,0),2) to handle users with no rows.
skeleton:
SELECT
  SUM(CASE WHEN action='confirmed' THEN 1 END) confirmed,
  COUNT(*) total,
  ROUND(COALESCE(SUM(CASE WHEN action='confirmed' THEN 1 END)/COUNT(*),0),2) rate
FROM Confirmations
gotcha: COALESCE the rate to 0.00 when a user has zero rows — NULL output fails the judge silently
script: Count or sum rows by multiple categories in one pass? Wrap each condition in a SUM of CASE WHEN to count only matching rows per group. One aggregation pass, no repeated table scans. The trap: when a user has zero qualifying rows the result is null not zero — wrap with COALESCE to get zero. Memory tag: CASE inside SUM is a pivot without PIVOT syntax.

trigger: output requires exactly two decimal places and NULLs must appear as 0.00 not NULL
move: ROUND(COALESCE(expr, 0), 2) handles both cases: COALESCE replaces NULL with 0 before ROUND applies the decimal formatting. Apply COALESCE first, ROUND second — reversing the order still rounds but NULL stays NULL.
example: AVG(salary) on an empty table returns NULL. ROUND(NULL, 2) = NULL which fails the judge. ROUND(COALESCE(AVG(salary), 0), 2) = 0.00 which passes. Same pattern for confirmation rates on users with no records.
skeleton:
SELECT ROUND(COALESCE(AVG(salary),0),2) AS avg_salary
FROM ...
gotcha: 0.00 vs NULL is a silent test failure — the output column type matters and NULL rows are often filtered by the judge
script: Output requires exactly two decimal places and null must show as zero point zero zero? Wrap with COALESCE first to replace null with zero, then apply ROUND with two decimal places. Reversing the order still rounds but leaves null as null. The trap: null and zero point zero zero look different to the judge — always COALESCE before ROUND. Memory tag: COALESCE then ROUND, in that order.

trigger: return the Nth distinct salary value, returning NULL if fewer than N distinct salaries exist
move: Use a nested MAX/subquery: SELECT MAX(salary) WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary)). For the Nth: wrap in a CTE with DENSE_RANK and filter WHERE rk=N. Wrapping the outer query in a SELECT ensures NULL is returned when no rows match instead of an empty result set.
example: Employee salaries [300,200,100]: second highest = MAX where salary < 300 = 200. If only [300] exists, MAX where salary < 300 returns NULL. For third highest with DENSE_RANK: rank [1,2,3] -> WHERE rk=3 returns 100.
skeleton:
SELECT MAX(salary) SecondHighestSalary
FROM Employee
WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM Employee)
-- wrap in outer SELECT for guaranteed NULL:
SELECT (
  SELECT MAX(salary) FROM Employee
  WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM Employee)
) AS SecondHighestSalary
gotcha: without an outer wrapper the query returns zero rows instead of a NULL row — many judges distinguish these
script: Return the Nth distinct salary with null when fewer than N exist? Use a nested MAX where salary is less than the top salary to get second highest. For arbitrary N use DENSE_RANK in a CTE and filter where rank equals N. The trap: without an outer SELECT wrapper the query returns zero rows instead of a null row — many judges distinguish those. Memory tag: wrap in a SELECT to guarantee a null row.

trigger: find rows where the value increased compared to the immediately preceding calendar day
move: Self-join the table aliased as yesterday and today on DATEDIFF(today.date, yesterday.date)=1. Then filter WHERE today.value > yesterday.value. Alternatively use LAG ordered by date, which is cleaner when dates are unique.
example: Weather table: 2015-01-02 temp=25, 2015-01-03 temp=30. DATEDIFF('2015-01-03','2015-01-02')=1 so they join. 30>25 so 2015-01-03's id is returned. If a date is missing, the join finds no match and that date is skipped.
skeleton:
SELECT w1.id
FROM Weather w1
JOIN Weather w2
  ON DATEDIFF(w1.recordDate,w2.recordDate)=1
WHERE w1.temperature > w2.temperature
gotcha: DATEDIFF(later, earlier) = positive in MySQL; reversing args gives a negative and the join matches nothing
script: Find rows where a value increased compared to exactly the prior calendar day? Self join the table on a date difference of exactly one and filter where today's value beats yesterday's. Or use LAG ordered by date which is cleaner. The trap: MySQL DATEDIFF takes later date first — swapping the arguments gives negative numbers and the join finds nothing. Memory tag: DATEDIFF later minus earlier equals positive.

trigger: you need a named intermediate result set referenced in multiple places within the same query
move: WITH name AS (SELECT ...) defines a temporary named result. Reference it like a table in the main query. CTEs improve readability over nested subqueries and allow the same derived table to be referenced multiple times without recomputing it in code (though the optimizer may or may not materialize it).
example: Find the highest salary per department, then join back to Employee to get the employee name: CTE dept_max computes MAX(salary) per deptId; the main query joins Employee to dept_max on both deptId and salary matching. This avoids a correlated subquery in the WHERE clause.
skeleton:
WITH dept_max AS (
  SELECT deptId, MAX(salary) max_sal
  FROM Employee GROUP BY deptId)
SELECT e.name, d.name, e.salary
FROM Employee e
JOIN Department d ON e.deptId=d.id
JOIN dept_max dm ON e.deptId=dm.deptId AND e.salary=dm.max_sal
gotcha: CTEs are not automatically cached — the optimizer may re-execute them each time they are referenced
script: Need to use the same derived result in multiple places in one query? Define it once with a WITH clause and give it a name. Reference it like a regular table anywhere below. The trap: the optimizer may re-execute the CTE each time it appears — it is not guaranteed to cache the result. Memory tag: WITH names a reusable subquery.

trigger: reshape row-level category data into one column per category within a single aggregated row
move: Wrap each category in SUM(CASE WHEN category='X' THEN value ELSE 0 END) AS X. GROUP BY the row identifier. Every category becomes its own column in a single pass. Works in all SQL dialects since native PIVOT syntax is absent in MySQL and PostgreSQL.
example: Monthly revenue stored as (id, month, revenue) rows: SUM(CASE WHEN month='Jan' THEN revenue END) Jan, SUM(CASE WHEN month='Feb' THEN revenue END) Feb, GROUP BY id. Id 1 with Jan=8000 and Feb=7000 becomes a single row [1, 8000, 7000].
skeleton:
SELECT id,
  SUM(CASE WHEN month='Jan' THEN revenue END) Jan,
  SUM(CASE WHEN month='Feb' THEN revenue END) Feb
FROM dept_revenue
GROUP BY id
gotcha: MySQL and PostgreSQL have no native PIVOT keyword — CASE aggregation is the universal portable approach
script: Turn row-per-category data into one column per category in a single aggregated row? Use SUM of CASE WHEN for each category column and GROUP BY the row identifier. Works in any SQL dialect. The trap: MySQL and PostgreSQL have no native PIVOT keyword so CASE aggregation is the universal approach. Memory tag: one CASE per column, GROUP BY the row key.

trigger: combine the results of two SELECT statements into one result set
move: UNION removes duplicates by running a DISTINCT pass on the combined set. UNION ALL keeps every row including duplicates and is faster because it skips the dedup step. Use UNION only when deduplication is actually required.
example: Low-fat AND recyclable products: UNION between WHERE low_fats='Y' and WHERE recyclable='Y'. If product 5 is both, UNION returns it once; UNION ALL returns it twice. For non-overlapping sets they produce identical results but UNION ALL is always faster.
skeleton:
SELECT product_id FROM Products WHERE low_fats='Y'
UNION ALL
SELECT product_id FROM Products WHERE recyclable='Y'
gotcha: UNION does a full DISTINCT sort on the result — use UNION ALL unless you actually need deduplication
script: Combining two SELECT results into one? UNION ALL keeps everything including duplicates and is fast. UNION deduplicates by running a sort on the combined result. The trap: if the sets do not overlap they produce the same output but UNION wastes time deduplicating. Memory tag: UNION ALL unless you need dedup.

trigger: generate every combination of rows from two tables as a scaffold, then fill in actual data with a LEFT JOIN
move: CROSS JOIN produces the Cartesian product of two tables (every row in A paired with every row in B). Use this as a complete grid, then LEFT JOIN the actual data onto it so that missing combinations show up as zero counts rather than being absent from the output.
example: Each student-subject pair with exam count: Students has Alice and Bob; Subjects has Math and Physics. CROSS JOIN gives 4 rows. LEFT JOIN Examinations fills in counts where exams exist. Alice with 0 Math exams still appears as (Alice, Math, 0) instead of being omitted.
skeleton:
SELECT s.student_id, s.student_name, sub.subject_name,
  COUNT(e.student_id) attended_exams
FROM Students s
CROSS JOIN Subjects sub
LEFT JOIN Examinations e
  ON s.student_id=e.student_id AND sub.subject_name=e.subject_name
GROUP BY s.student_id,s.student_name,sub.subject_name
gotcha: the CROSS JOIN must come before the LEFT JOIN so every combination exists before matching actual data
script: Need every combination of two sets as a starting grid, including ones with no real data? CROSS JOIN produces the full Cartesian product — every row from the first table paired with every row from the second. Then LEFT JOIN actual data onto that grid so missing combos show up as zero rather than disappearing. Memory tag: CROSS JOIN first for the grid, LEFT JOIN for the data.

tag: NOT IN vs NOT EXISTS NULL trap
trigger: writing an anti-join when the subquery column might contain NULL values
move: NOT IN evaluates as unknown (not true) when any value in the subquery list is NULL, causing the entire WHERE clause to return no rows. NOT EXISTS uses correlated subquery logic that is NULL-safe because it only checks row existence, not value equality.
example: Orders.customerId has a NULL entry. WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT customerId FROM Orders) returns zero rows because NULL IN (1,2,NULL) = UNKNOWN, so NOT IN = NOT UNKNOWN = UNKNOWN, which fails the WHERE filter. NOT EXISTS returns the correct customer list.
skeleton:
-- BROKEN if customerId has NULLs:
WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT customerId FROM Orders)
-- SAFE:
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM Orders WHERE customerId=c.id)
gotcha: NULL IN (1,2,NULL) evaluates to NULL, not False — the entire NOT IN anti-join silently returns zero rows
script: Writing an anti-join where the subquery column might have null values? NOT IN evaluates to unknown when any value in the list is null, so the entire WHERE clause silently returns zero rows. NOT EXISTS checks row existence, not value equality, and handles nulls correctly. Memory tag: NOT IN plus null equals nothing — use NOT EXISTS.

trigger: aggregate multiple string values from different rows into a single delimited string per group
move: MySQL: GROUP_CONCAT(col ORDER BY col SEPARATOR ',') collapses all values in the group into one string. PostgreSQL/SQL Server: STRING_AGG(col, ',' ORDER BY col) does the same. These are the only portable options for row-to-string aggregation.
example: Employees in each department: SELECT dept, GROUP_CONCAT(name ORDER BY name) FROM employees GROUP BY dept returns 'Engineering: Alice,Bob,Carol'. PostgreSQL: STRING_AGG(name, ',' ORDER BY name) produces identical output.
skeleton:
-- MySQL:
SELECT dept, GROUP_CONCAT(name ORDER BY name) members
FROM employees GROUP BY dept
-- PostgreSQL:
SELECT dept, STRING_AGG(name,',' ORDER BY name) FROM employees GROUP BY dept
gotcha: MySQL GROUP_CONCAT truncates at 1024 chars by default — SET group_concat_max_len=10000 if you need longer strings
script: Need to collapse many rows of strings into one delimited value per group? In MySQL use GROUP_CONCAT with an ORDER BY and SEPARATOR option inside the function. In PostgreSQL use STRING_AGG with a delimiter and ORDER BY. The trap: MySQL silently truncates at one thousand twenty four characters by default — raise the limit if you need longer strings. Memory tag: GROUP_CONCAT in MySQL, STRING_AGG in Postgres.

trigger: delete duplicate rows from a table keeping only one representative row per unique key
move: Self-join the table on the duplicate key column where the id of one row is greater than the other. DELETE the row with the higher id. Alternatively use a ROW_NUMBER CTE and delete WHERE rn > 1.
example: Person(id, email) with rows (1,a@b.com),(2,a@b.com),(3,c@d.com): self-join on p1.email=p2.email AND p1.id>p2.id matches row 2 (id=2 > id=1). DELETE p1 removes row 2. Row 1 and 3 survive.
skeleton:
DELETE p1 FROM Person p1
JOIN Person p2
  ON p1.email=p2.email AND p1.id>p2.id
gotcha: MySQL disallows SELECT from the same table being deleted in a subquery — the self-join form avoids this restriction
script: Delete duplicate rows from a table keeping the row with the lowest ID? Self join the table on the duplicate key where the first alias has a higher ID than the second, then delete the first alias. Or use ROW_NUMBER in a CTE and delete where rank is greater than one. The trap: MySQL disallows selecting from the same table you are deleting in a subquery — the self join form avoids this. Memory tag: self join, delete the higher ID.

trigger: compute a rolling N-day sum or average where the window slides across ordered rows
move: SUM(col) OVER (ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN N-1 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) accumulates across exactly N physical rows. The ROWS BETWEEN clause defines the frame as a fixed row count, not a date range, so calendar gaps affect results.
example: 7-day rolling visitor spend: SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY visited_on ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW). On 2019-01-07 this sums rows at positions -6 through 0 relative to current. If 2019-01-03 has no data (missing row), only 6 physical rows exist and the sum covers fewer than 7 days.
skeleton:
SELECT visited_on,
  SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY visited_on
    ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) total
FROM Customer
gotcha: ROWS BETWEEN counts physical rows, not calendar days — a gap in dates means the window covers more than 7 days of calendar time
script: Compute a rolling sum over a fixed number of rows rather than a date range? Use SUM with a window frame of N minus one rows preceding to current row. Each row then sums exactly N physical rows. The trap: ROWS BETWEEN counts physical rows not calendar days — a missing date means the window covers more calendar days than expected. Memory tag: ROWS BETWEEN counts rows not dates.

trigger: asked how surprising your experiment result is, or whether to reject the null hypothesis
move: The p-value is the probability of observing data at least as extreme as what you got, assuming the null hypothesis is true. It is not the probability that the null is true or false. A small p-value means the data is surprising under the null, so you reject it.
example: You test whether a coin is fair. You flip 100 times and get 65 heads. Under H0 (p=0.5), a z-score of (65-50)/5=3 gives p≈0.003. That means only 0.3% of fair-coin experiments would produce >=65 heads. With alpha=0.05, reject H0.
gotcha: p < 0.05 does not prove the alternative is true — it only says the data is unlikely under the null
script: How surprising is your result if the null hypothesis were true? That is your p-value — the probability of seeing data at least this extreme assuming nothing is going on. Small means surprising, not that the null is false. The trap: p below zero point zero five does not prove the alternative true, it just says the data is unlikely under the null. Memory tag: p-value is surprise under null, not proof.

tag: Type I vs Type II error
trigger: asked what a false positive or false negative means in the context of hypothesis testing
move: Type I error: you reject a null hypothesis that is actually true (false alarm). Type II error: you fail to reject a null hypothesis that is actually false (missed signal). Alpha is the tolerated Type I rate; power (1-beta) is the probability of correctly detecting a real effect.
example: Drug trial: Type I = conclude the drug works when it does not (approve a useless drug). Type II = conclude the drug doesn't work when it does (reject a helpful drug). Lowering alpha (stricter threshold) reduces Type I but increases Type II risk.
gotcha: lowering alpha reduces false positives but requires larger sample size to maintain power and avoid more false negatives
script: False alarm or missed signal — which is which? Type one is a false alarm: you reject a true null. Type two is a missed signal: you fail to reject a false null. Alpha controls how often you tolerate a false alarm. The trap: tightening alpha to reduce false positives increases missed signals unless you raise the sample size. Memory tag: type one fires when null is innocent, type two stays quiet when null is guilty.

trigger: asked why we can use normal-distribution tests even when the underlying data isn't normally distributed
move: The Central Limit Theorem says the distribution of sample means approaches normal as sample size n grows, regardless of the shape of the underlying population distribution. This is what makes z-tests and t-tests valid for large samples from non-normal populations.
example: Die rolls are uniformly distributed on 1-6, mean=3.5, std≈1.71. Take 30 samples, compute the mean. Do this 10000 times. The distribution of those sample means will look nearly bell-shaped around 3.5 with std=1.71/sqrt(30)≈0.31.
gotcha: CLT applies to the distribution of the SAMPLE MEAN, not individual observations — n>=30 is a rule of thumb, not a guarantee
script: Why can you use normal-distribution tests on non-normal data? Because the Central Limit Theorem says sample means approach normal as sample size grows, regardless of the underlying shape. This is what makes t-tests valid for large samples. The trap: CLT applies to the distribution of the sample mean, not individual data points. Memory tag: means go normal, observations do not have to.

trigger: asked to interpret a 95% confidence interval or explain what confidence level means
move: A 95% CI means: if you repeated the study many times and built a CI each time, approximately 95% of those intervals would contain the true population parameter. It says nothing about the probability that the true value lies in this specific interval — it either does or it doesn't.
example: You estimate mean height as 170cm with 95% CI [168, 172]. This does not mean there is a 95% chance the true mean is between 168 and 172. It means the procedure that built this interval captures the true mean 95% of the time across repeated experiments.
gotcha: do NOT say 'there is a 95% chance the true mean is in [168,172]' — the true mean is fixed; the interval is random
script: What does a ninety-five percent confidence interval actually mean? If you repeated the study and built a new interval each time, ninety-five percent of those intervals would contain the true parameter. It does not mean there is a ninety-five percent chance the truth is inside this particular interval. The trap: the true value is fixed — the interval is what varies. Memory tag: the procedure captures truth ninety-five percent of the time, not this specific interval.

trigger: choosing between mean and median as a summary statistic when data might be skewed or have outliers
move: Outliers pull the mean toward them but do not affect the median. For right-skewed distributions (income, house prices), the median is the better central tendency measure. Use mean only when the distribution is roughly symmetric and free of extreme values.
example: Incomes in a neighborhood: [30k, 35k, 40k, 45k, 500k]. Mean=130k, median=40k. The 500k outlier inflates the mean to a value no one actually earns. Median 40k better represents the typical resident.
gotcha: mean can look normal even with a single outlier — always check a histogram or box plot before choosing
script: Skewed data or outliers — should you report mean or median? Outliers pull the mean toward them but barely touch the median. For income, house prices, or any right-skewed distribution the median better represents the typical case. The trap: a single extreme outlier can inflate the mean far past where anyone actually sits. Memory tag: median ignores outliers, mean does not.

trigger: explain the relationship between variance and standard deviation and when to use each
move: Variance is the average of squared deviations from the mean. Standard deviation is the square root of variance, which puts the measure back in the original units of the data, making it directly interpretable. For comparing spread across datasets, std is more intuitive.
example: Heights in cm: [160,165,170,175,180], mean=170. Deviations: [-10,-5,0,5,10]. Squared: [100,25,0,25,100]. Variance (population)=50. Std=sqrt(50)=7.07 cm. So a typical person is about 7 cm from the mean.
gotcha: sample variance divides by n-1 (Bessel's correction) to correct for underestimating population variance
script: What is the difference between variance and standard deviation? Variance is the average of squared deviations from the mean, which puts the result in squared units. Standard deviation is its square root, which brings the measure back into the original units and makes it directly interpretable. The trap: sample variance divides by n minus one, not n, to correct for underestimation. Memory tag: std is sqrt of variance, back in original units.

trigger: two variables are correlated and someone wants to conclude that one causes the other
move: Correlation measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship. But a third variable (confounder) might drive both, or causality could run in reverse. You need a controlled experiment or causal inference methods to establish causation.
example: Ice cream sales and drowning rates both spike in summer (r≈0.85). Temperature causes both. Concluding 'ice cream causes drowning' is the confounder fallacy. Similarly, countries with more TVs per capita have higher life expectancy — wealth drives both.
gotcha: high r^2 in a regression does not prove causation — it only says the linear fit is good on observed data
script: Two things move together — does one cause the other? Not necessarily. A hidden third variable often drives both. Ice cream sales and drowning both spike in summer because heat causes both, not because ice cream is dangerous. The trap: high R-squared in a regression means good fit, not that you found a cause. Memory tag: correlation shows a link, not a direction.

trigger: computing the probability of A given that B has already occurred, or updating a prior belief with new evidence
move: P(A|B) = P(A and B) / P(B). Bayes' theorem rewrites this as P(A|B) = P(B|A)*P(A)/P(B), letting you flip the conditioning when P(B|A) is easier to compute than P(A|B) directly.
example: Disease test: 1% of population has the disease (P(D)=0.01). Test is 99% sensitive (P(+|D)=0.99) and 95% specific (P(-|no D)=0.95). P(D|+) = (0.99*0.01) / (0.99*0.01 + 0.05*0.99) ≈ 0.167. Only 16.7% chance of having disease given a positive test.
gotcha: P(A|B) is almost never equal to P(B|A) — the prosecutor's fallacy confuses P(evidence|innocent) with P(innocent|evidence)
script: Need to flip a conditional probability? Use Bayes. The probability of A given B equals the probability of B given A times the prior for A, divided by the total probability of B. This lets you update a belief with new evidence. The trap: the probability of A given B almost never equals the probability of B given A — mixing them up is the prosecutor's fallacy. Memory tag: Bayes flips the conditioning.

trigger: asked what properties a bell curve has or what percent of data falls within k standard deviations
move: The normal distribution is symmetric about its mean. Mean = median = mode. The 68-95-99.7 rule: 68% of data falls within 1 std dev, 95% within 2, and 99.7% within 3. Z-scores standardize any normal variable to mean 0, std 1.
example: IQ is N(100, 15). 68% of people score between 85 and 115 (100 +/- 15). 95% score between 70 and 130. Only 0.3% score below 55 or above 145. A score of 130 has z-score=(130-100)/15=2, meaning it's at the 97.7th percentile.
gotcha: real data is rarely perfectly normal — always check with a histogram or Q-Q plot before applying normal-based tests
script: Bell curve properties everyone should know: the normal distribution is symmetric around its mean, and mean equals median equals mode. Sixty-eight percent of data falls within one standard deviation, ninety-five within two, ninety-nine point seven within three. The trap: real data is rarely perfectly normal — check a histogram before applying normal-based tests. Memory tag: sixty-eight, ninety-five, ninety-nine point seven.

trigger: your sample was collected in a way that systematically over- or under-represents some part of the population
move: Sampling bias occurs when the method of selecting participants makes some individuals more likely to be included than others. This distorts estimates because the sample no longer represents the target population. Larger samples do not fix a biased sampling method.
example: Surveying app user satisfaction via an in-app popup: only users currently engaged with the app respond, over-representing happy users and missing churned users. Even with n=10000 the estimate is biased high. Random sampling from the full user base is the fix.
gotcha: larger n does not reduce bias — it only reduces variance around the biased estimate
script: Your sample was collected in a way that favors certain respondents? That is sampling bias and more data will not fix it — you will just get a more precise wrong answer. The bias is in the collection method, not the sample size. The trap: a survey of current users excludes churned users — you are systematically missing the unhappy people. Memory tag: bigger n shrinks variance, not bias.

trigger: you have collected data and want to formally test a claim about a population parameter
move: Follow this sequence: state H0 and H1, set significance level alpha (typically 0.05), choose the correct test statistic, compute the test statistic and its p-value, then reject H0 if p < alpha. Failing to reject H0 is not the same as accepting it.
example: Claim: average delivery time = 30 min. Sample of 50 deliveries has mean=32 min, std=8 min. H0: mu=30. t-stat=(32-30)/(8/sqrt(50))=1.77. With df=49 the two-tailed p≈0.083 > 0.05. Fail to reject H0 — not enough evidence to say delivery time differs from 30 min.
gotcha: 'fail to reject' is NOT 'accept the null' — the data is just insufficient to disprove it at the given alpha
script: Running a formal hypothesis test? State null and alternative, set alpha, pick the right test statistic, compute the p-value, then reject if p is below alpha. Failing to reject is not the same as accepting the null — the data is just insufficient to disprove it. Memory tag: fail to reject is not accept.

trigger: asked to describe or compare how spread out two datasets are around their means
move: Variance quantifies spread by averaging the squared deviations from the mean. High variance means values are dispersed far from the mean; low variance means they cluster tightly. A single extreme outlier inflates variance disproportionately because deviations are squared.
example: Dataset A: [10,10,10,10], variance=0. Dataset B: [8,9,11,12], variance=2.5. Dataset C: [1,10,10,19], variance=40.5. C has the same mean as B (10) but C's outliers at 1 and 19 explode the variance.
gotcha: one extreme outlier can dominate variance because the squared term amplifies large deviations nonlinearly
script: How spread out is a dataset? Variance averages the squared deviations from the mean. High variance means values scatter far; low variance means they cluster tightly. The trap: a single extreme outlier explodes variance because squaring amplifies large deviations. Memory tag: one outlier dominates variance, because squaring is brutal.

trigger: you need to decide which class of learning algorithm fits a problem based on whether labels exist
move: Supervised learning uses labeled training data (input-output pairs) to learn a mapping — used for regression (predict a number) and classification (predict a category). Unsupervised learning finds patterns in unlabeled data — used for clustering, dimensionality reduction, and anomaly detection.
example: Predicting whether an email is spam (yes/no label exists) = supervised classification. Grouping customers by purchase behavior with no predefined categories = unsupervised clustering. Predicting house price from features (labeled dataset of past sales) = supervised regression.
gotcha: k-means is unsupervised; logistic regression and random forests are supervised — the label requirement is the dividing line
script: Does your data come with labels? Supervised learning trains on input-output pairs for regression and classification. Unsupervised learning finds structure in unlabeled data through clustering or dimensionality reduction. The trap: k-means is unsupervised; logistic regression and random forests are supervised — the presence of labels is the dividing line. Memory tag: labels mean supervised, no labels mean unsupervised.

trigger: training accuracy is high but validation or test accuracy is significantly lower
move: Overfitting means the model learned the noise and specific patterns in the training data rather than generalizable rules. It has high variance and low bias. Fixes include getting more training data, reducing model complexity, adding regularization (L1/L2), or using dropout for neural networks.
example: A decision tree with no depth limit on 500 training samples reaches 100% train accuracy but 62% test accuracy. Limiting max_depth to 5 gives 85% train and 83% test accuracy — much better generalization. The deep tree memorized the training set.
gotcha: training longer does NOT fix overfitting — it makes it worse; you need regularization or simpler model architecture
script: Training accuracy great but test accuracy is way lower? That is overfitting — the model learned the noise instead of the signal. It has high variance and low bias. Fixes include more data, simpler model, or regularization. The trap: training longer makes it worse, not better. Memory tag: overtrained on noise, falls apart on new data.

trigger: asked to explain why a model underperforms and whether it's too simple or too complex
move: High bias (underfitting): the model is too simple to capture the true pattern — both train and test error are high. High variance (overfitting): the model is too complex and learns noise — train error is low but test error is high. The goal is a model complex enough to fit the signal but not so complex it learns noise.
example: Polynomial regression on noisy data: degree 1 (linear) has high bias — misses the curve. Degree 15 has high variance — perfectly fits training noise, performs poorly on new points. Degree 3 hits the sweet spot. Cross-validation identifies the best degree empirically.
gotcha: you cannot simultaneously minimize both bias and variance — reducing one tends to increase the other
script: Is your model underperforming because it is too simple or too complex? High bias means both train and test error are high — model is too simple. High variance means train is low but test is high — model is too complex. The goal is the sweet spot in between. The trap: reducing one always tends to increase the other. Memory tag: simple means bias, complex means variance.

trigger: evaluating a classifier on an imbalanced dataset where false positives and false negatives have different costs
move: Precision = TP/(TP+FP): of all the times you predicted positive, how often were you right? Recall = TP/(TP+FN): of all the actual positives, how many did you catch? High precision minimizes false alarms; high recall minimizes missed cases. The threshold controls the tradeoff.
example: Cancer screening: precision 0.5 means half your positive predictions are wrong (unnecessary biopsies); recall 0.95 means you catch 95% of actual cancers. For a life-threatening disease you want high recall even at the cost of lower precision. For spam filters, high precision matters more (don't lose real emails).
gotcha: raising the decision threshold increases precision but decreases recall — you cannot maximize both simultaneously
script: Classifier on imbalanced data — which metric matters? Precision is how often your positive predictions are correct. Recall is how many real positives you caught. High precision minimizes false alarms; high recall minimizes missed cases. The trap: raising the decision threshold increases precision but drops recall — you cannot maximize both at once. Memory tag: precision is about your calls, recall is about the truth.

trigger: you need a single metric that balances precision and recall on a class-imbalanced dataset
move: F1 = 2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall). It is the harmonic mean, which penalizes extreme imbalances between precision and recall. A model with 1.0 precision but 0.1 recall gets F1=0.18, not 0.55 — the harmonic mean forces both to be high.
example: Model A: precision=0.9, recall=0.1 -> F1=0.18. Model B: precision=0.7, recall=0.7 -> F1=0.70. Model B is far better by F1 despite lower precision. On a 99/1 imbalanced dataset, a model predicting all-negative gets accuracy=99% but F1=0 because recall=0.
gotcha: accuracy is useless on imbalanced datasets — a model predicting all-majority gets high accuracy but F1 of zero
script: Need one number that balances precision and recall? F1 is the harmonic mean of the two. It punishes extreme imbalances — a model with perfect precision but near-zero recall gets an F1 close to zero, not close to one. The trap: accuracy is useless on imbalanced datasets because predicting all-majority gives high accuracy with zero recall. Memory tag: harmonic mean, both must be high.

trigger: you want to penalize large model weights to reduce overfitting and need to choose between Lasso and Ridge
move: L1 (Lasso) adds the sum of absolute weight values to the loss. It drives some weights exactly to zero, effectively doing feature selection. L2 (Ridge) adds the sum of squared weights, which shrinks all weights toward zero but rarely zeros them out — it retains all features with small coefficients.
example: 10-feature regression: L1 with alpha=0.1 might zero out 6 features, leaving a sparse 4-feature model that is interpretable. L2 with alpha=0.1 keeps all 10 features but shrinks the noisy ones to near-zero. Use L1 when you suspect many irrelevant features; L2 when all features may contribute.
gotcha: L1 is not differentiable at zero so gradient descent requires a subgradient; L2 has a closed-form normal equation solution
script: Want to shrink model weights to fight overfitting — L1 or L2? L1 adds the sum of absolute weights to the loss and drives some weights exactly to zero, doing feature selection. L2 adds squared weights and shrinks all of them toward zero without zeroing them out. The trap: L1 is not smooth at zero so gradient descent needs a subgradient; L2 has a closed-form solution. Memory tag: L1 zeroes features out, L2 just shrinks them.

trigger: you have limited data and need a reliable estimate of model generalization performance
move: K-fold CV splits data into k equal folds. Train on k-1 folds, evaluate on the held-out fold, repeat k times, then average the scores. This gives a lower-variance performance estimate than a single train/test split and uses all data for both training and evaluation across folds.
example: 5-fold CV on 500 samples: fold 1 tests on rows 1-100, folds 2-5 each test on 100 different rows. Accuracy scores: [0.82, 0.85, 0.83, 0.84, 0.81]. Mean=0.83, std=0.015. More reliable than a single 80/20 split which might land on an unrepresentative split.
gotcha: never preprocess (scale, encode) the full dataset before splitting — that leaks test info into training, inflating performance estimates
script: Limited data and need a reliable performance estimate? K-fold cross-validation splits the data into K folds, trains on K minus one, evaluates on the held-out fold, and rotates until every fold has been the test set. Average the scores. The trap: never scale or encode the full dataset before splitting — that leaks test information into training and inflates your numbers. Memory tag: rotate the held-out fold, average the results.

trigger: you need to understand all four types of classification outcomes for a binary classifier
move: A 2x2 confusion matrix shows: TP (correctly predicted positive), TN (correctly predicted negative), FP (predicted positive but actually negative), FN (predicted negative but actually positive). All standard metrics derive from these four counts.
example: Spam detector on 100 emails (30 spam, 70 ham): TP=25 (caught spam), TN=65 (correct ham), FP=5 (ham flagged as spam), FN=5 (spam missed). Precision=25/30=0.83, Recall=25/30=0.83, Accuracy=90/100=0.90.
gotcha: high accuracy does not mean a good classifier — on a 95/5 split a model predicting all-negative gets 95% accuracy with 0 TP
script: Need to understand all four outcome types for a binary classifier? True positives and true negatives are correct calls. False positives are false alarms. False negatives are missed cases. All standard metrics — precision, recall, accuracy — derive from these four counts. The trap: high accuracy on an imbalanced dataset still means zero true positives if the model never predicts the minority class. Memory tag: TP TN FP FN, everything else derives from these.

trigger: a model reports 99% accuracy on a dataset where one class dominates — is it actually good
move: On a 99/1 class split, a model that always predicts the majority class achieves 99% accuracy while being completely useless for detecting the minority class. Accuracy = (TP+TN)/total is misleading here. Use precision, recall, F1, or AUC-ROC instead.
example: Fraud detection: 99% of transactions are legitimate. A model that never flags fraud gets 99% accuracy, 0% recall, and F1=0. It catches zero fraudulent transactions. Switching to F1 or AUC exposes this immediately.
gotcha: always sanity-check a high accuracy number against the baseline of predicting the majority class
script: Model reports ninety-nine percent accuracy — is it actually good? Check the baseline: a model that always predicts the majority class on a ninety-nine to one split achieves ninety-nine percent accuracy with zero useful predictions. Accuracy is misleading on imbalanced data. Use precision, recall, F1, or AUC-ROC instead. Memory tag: majority-class baseline always exposes the accuracy trap.

trigger: determine whether a problem calls for a regression model or a classification model
move: Regression predicts a continuous numeric output (price, temperature, revenue). Classification predicts a discrete label or category (spam/not, dog/cat/bird). The choice determines your loss function, evaluation metric, and output activation (sigmoid/softmax for classification, linear for regression).
example: Predicting the exact price of a house is regression (output: $342,000). Predicting whether a price is above or below the median is binary classification. Predicting neighborhood tier (luxury/mid/budget) is multi-class classification.
gotcha: logistic regression has 'regression' in the name but it is a classification algorithm — its output is a class probability
script: Is your output a number or a category? Regression predicts a continuous value like price or temperature. Classification predicts a discrete label like spam or not spam. The choice determines your loss function, your metric, and your output layer. The trap: logistic regression has regression in the name but it is a classification algorithm. Memory tag: number out is regression, category out is classification.

trigger: group unlabeled data points into clusters based on similarity with no predefined categories
move: K-means: randomly initialize k centroids, assign each point to the nearest centroid, recompute each centroid as the mean of its assigned points, repeat until assignments stop changing. The algorithm minimizes within-cluster variance (inertia).
example: Customer segmentation with k=3: iteration 1 centroids at random positions; after assignment step, cluster 1 has high-spend customers, cluster 2 has low-frequency buyers, cluster 3 has discount shoppers; centroids shift to the mean of each group; repeat until stable.
gotcha: k-means requires k specified upfront and is sensitive to initialization — run multiple times with different random seeds and pick the lowest inertia
script: No labels, want to group similar data points? K-means randomly places K centroids, assigns each point to the nearest one, recomputes each centroid as the mean of its cluster, and repeats until assignments stop changing. The trap: k-means is sensitive to initialization — run it multiple times with different starting points and keep the lowest inertia run. Memory tag: assign to nearest centroid, recompute, repeat.

trigger: you need to evaluate and tune a model while keeping a truly held-out set for honest final reporting
move: Training set: fit model weights. Validation set: tune hyperparameters and select architecture. Test set: report final performance exactly once. Touching the test set during development causes optimistic bias because you implicitly tune to it.
example: Dataset of 10000 examples: 7000 train, 1500 val, 1500 test. Train multiple models, pick the one with best val accuracy (say 84%). Report the chosen model's test accuracy (say 83%). That 83% is your honest estimate — not the 84% seen during selection.
gotcha: using test set accuracy to choose hyperparameters makes it a second validation set — your final reported metric is then optimistic
script: How do you keep your test set honest? Training set fits the weights. Validation set tunes hyperparameters. Test set gets touched exactly once at the very end to report final performance. The trap: using test accuracy to pick hyperparameters makes it a second validation set — your reported number is then optimistic. Memory tag: test set is touched once, at the end.

trigger: Python // operator gives unexpected result on negative numbers — floors instead of truncates
move: Python's // operator floors toward negative infinity, so -7//2 = -4 not -3. The / operator always returns a float. When a problem requires truncation toward zero (like LeetCode's Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation), use int(a/b) instead of a//b.
example: -7//2 = -4 (floors down). int(-7/2) = int(-3.5) = -3 (truncates toward zero). For positive numbers both give 3. The difference only surfaces on negative operands. RPN problem requires int(-3.5)=-3, not -4.
skeleton:
print(7//2)   # 3
print(-7//2)  # -4 (floors, not truncates)
gotcha: RPN (LeetCode 150) requires truncation toward zero — use int(b/a) not b//a for the division case
script: Python double-slash integer division on negative numbers — not what you expect. Double-slash floors toward negative infinity, so negative seven divided by two gives negative four, not negative three. For truncation toward zero like most language specs require, use int of the division instead. The trap: positive numbers look identical with both approaches — the difference only shows up on negatives. Memory tag: double-slash floors, int of divide truncates.

trigger: binary search loop terminates early or runs forever due to a wrong condition or mid-pointer update
move: Use while l<=r (inclusive) not while l<r. Compute mid as l+(r-l)//2. Always update to l=mid+1 or r=mid-1, never l=mid or r=mid. If you set r=mid instead of r=mid-1 and a[mid]>target, the loop can cycle forever on a two-element array.
example: a=[2,5], t=3: l=0,r=1,mid=0, a[0]=2<3 so l=1; mid=1, a[1]=5>3 so r=0; now l>r so exit. Return -1. If we had set r=mid=1 instead of r=0, next iteration still has l=1,r=1,mid=1 — infinite loop.
skeleton:
l,r=0,len(a)-1
while l<=r:
  m=l+(r-l)//2
  # set l=m+1 or r=m-1, never m
gotcha: forgetting the +1 or -1 in the pointer update causes infinite loops on two-element inputs
script: Binary search loop runs forever or exits too early? Use while left less than or equal to right. Always update left to mid plus one or right to mid minus one — never to mid itself. The trap: setting right to mid instead of mid minus one on a two-element array causes the loop to cycle forever. Memory tag: always plus one or minus one, never mid itself.

trigger: your function crashes or returns a wrong answer when the input list is empty or has exactly one element
move: Add guards at the top: if not nums: return 0 (or [], or False depending on return type). Handle the single-element case explicitly when your algorithm assumes at least two elements. Test these cases mentally before submitting.
example: Kadane's max subarray initialized with nums[0]: if nums=[] and you access nums[0] you get IndexError. Guard with if not nums: return 0. For [5] the loop body never executes and the initial value 5 is returned correctly.
skeleton:
if not nums: return 0
if len(nums)==1: return nums[0]
gotcha: single-element edge cases expose off-by-one bugs more than any other input — always trace through manually
script: Function crashes or returns wrong on empty or single-element input? Add a guard at the top before your main logic. Empty list should return a neutral value; single element sometimes needs its own branch. The trap: algorithms seeded with index zero or that assume at least two elements silently blow up on these inputs. Memory tag: guard empty and single before you do anything else.

trigger: def f(x, acc=[]) — the default list accumulates values across multiple calls unexpectedly
move: Python evaluates default argument values once when the function is defined, not on each call. A mutable default (list or dict) is shared across all calls. Use None as the default and initialize the mutable object inside the function body.
